passageText:
The philosophy of time has generated some of the most persistent puzzles in Western thought. Augustine's famous confession—'What then is time? If no one asks me, I know what it is. If I wish to explain it to him who asks, I do not know'—captures the paradox that pervades the field. Time is the medium in which we live and through which all change occurs, yet its nature resists systematic analysis.

The most fundamental division in the philosophy of time is between the A-series and the B-series, a distinction introduced by J.M.E. McTaggart in 1908. The A-series describes events as past, present, or future—positions that change as time flows. The B-series describes events as earlier than, simultaneous with, or later than one another—fixed, unchanging relations. McTaggart argued that the A-series is essential to time because it captures time's dynamic, flowing character; but the A-series generates a vicious regress: each event is past, present, and future (at different moments), and this requires a second time series to locate the event's A-determinations, and so on infinitely. He concluded that time is unreal.

Most philosophers have not followed McTaggart into unreality, but his distinction has shaped the debate. B-theorists (also called 'four-dimensionalists' or defenders of 'eternalism') argue that the A-series is an illusion generated by the subjective experience of a conscious being moving through a static four-dimensional spacetime. All times are equally real; 'now' is no more objectively privileged than 'here.' A-theorists (presentists and growing-block theorists) insist that temporal becoming—the genuine passage of time—is an objective feature of reality, not merely a psychological impression.

The debate intersects with physics in complex ways. Einstein's special theory of relativity, with its relativity of simultaneity, seems to support B-theory: there is no absolute 'now' that all observers share. Events that are simultaneous for one observer may be sequential for another, suggesting that the present has no privileged objective status. Yet some physicists and philosophers argue that quantum mechanics—particularly the measurement problem and the collapse of the wave function—implies a genuine directionality to time that the B-theory's static block universe cannot accommodate.

The phenomenology of time—how time is experienced by conscious beings—adds another dimension. Husserl's analysis of time-consciousness showed that experience is never a knife-edge present: we experience a thick 'specious present' that retains the just-past and anticipates the about-to-be, creating the experience of continuity and flow. Whether this phenomenological present corresponds to anything in the physical world or is entirely a product of neural processing remains contested.

questions.text: McTaggart's argument that time is unreal concludes from which logical problem?
options: The B-series fails to capture change

The A-series generates a vicious infinite regress: explaining how one event can have all three A-determinations (past, present, future) requires a second time series, which generates the same problem again
Einstein's relativity proves that no moment is objectively 'now'
The phenomenological present is too brief to anchor temporal experience
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'McTaggart argued that the A-series is essential to time because it captures time's dynamic, flowing character; but the A-series generates a vicious regress: each event is past, present, and future (at different moments), and this requires a second time series to locate the event's A-determinations, and so on infinitely. He concluded that time is unreal.'

questions.text: B-theorists differ from A-theorists in that B-theorists hold:
options: That time genuinely flows and the present is objectively privileged
That all times are equally real in a static four-dimensional spacetime and 'now' is merely a subjective experience, not an objective feature of reality
That quantum mechanics proves the direction of time
That Augustine's paradox shows time cannot be analyzed philosophically
questions.explanation: The passage states B-theorists 'argue that the A-series is an illusion generated by the subjective experience of a conscious being moving through a static four-dimensional spacetime. All times are equally real; now is no more objectively privileged than here.'

questions.text: Special relativity is cited in the passage as evidence that supports which position?
options: A-theory, because relativity shows that time genuinely passes
B-theory, because the relativity of simultaneity means there is no absolute 'now' shared by all observers — the present has no privileged objective status
McTaggart's position that time is unreal
Husserl's phenomenological analysis of the specious present
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Einstein's special theory of relativity, with its relativity of simultaneity, seems to support B-theory: there is no absolute now that all observers share... suggesting that the present has no privileged objective status.'

questions.text: Husserl's analysis of the 'specious present' contributes to the philosophy of time by showing:
options: That conscious experience confirms the B-theory's static block universe

That conscious experience is never a knife-edge present — it involves retention of the just-past and anticipation of the about-to-be, creating experienced continuity and flow; whether this corresponds to anything in physics is uncertain
That phenomenological analysis is irrelevant to the metaphysics of time
That quantum mechanics and consciousness are identical processes
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Husserl's analysis of time-consciousness showed that experience is never a knife-edge present: we experience a thick specious present that retains the just-past and anticipates the about-to-be, creating the experience of continuity and flow. Whether this phenomenological present corresponds to anything in the physical world or is entirely a product of neural processing remains contested.'

questions.text: The passage suggests that the debate between A-theory and B-theory is:
options: Settled in favor of B-theory by Einstein's relativity

Unresolved — relativity supports B-theory, but some argue quantum mechanics implies genuine temporal directionality supporting A-theory; both physical and phenomenological considerations remain contested
Irrelevant to physics and purely philosophical
Settled in favor of A-theory by quantum mechanics
questions.explanation: The passage presents both sides: relativity 'seems to support B-theory,' but 'some physicists and philosophers argue that quantum mechanics...implies a genuine directionality to time that the B-theory's static block universe cannot accommodate.' The debate 'remains contested.'

questions.text: Augustine's opening quotation ('What then is time? If no one asks me, I know...') is used in the passage to:
options: Prove that time is unknowable and all philosophical analysis is futile
Illustrate the paradox that time is experientially familiar but theoretically resistant to systematic analysis — setting up the philosophical puzzles the passage explores
Show that religious thinkers understood time better than scientists
Introduce the A-series/B-series distinction
questions.explanation: The passage uses Augustine's quotation to 'capture the paradox that pervades the field' — that time is 'the medium in which we live' yet 'its nature resists systematic analysis.' It introduces the topic and its central puzzle rather than making a claim about the limits of philosophy.

passageText:
The history of colonial medicine reveals the dual face of Western scientific expansion: the genuine amelioration of disease and suffering, alongside the imposition of racial hierarchies, extraction of biological knowledge, and destruction of indigenous healing systems. Understanding this history is essential not only for historians but for contemporary practitioners navigating the legacies that colonial medicine has bequeathed to global health.

Colonial medical systems did not simply arrive in conquered territories; they were produced in and through colonialism. Tropical medicine emerged as a discipline precisely to manage the health of European bodies in tropical environments and, increasingly, to maintain the productivity of colonized labor forces. Patrick Manson's discovery of the mosquito vector for malaria and Ronald Ross's confirmation of the Plasmodium lifecycle—advances that earned Ross a Nobel Prize—emerged from research conducted on colonized subjects whose consent was neither sought nor considered a relevant moral category.

The relationship between colonialism and race science was intimate. Physical anthropologists measured skulls and catalogued 'racial' differences in disease susceptibility, supposedly proving biological hierarchies that justified differential treatment and resource allocation. The Tuskegee syphilis study (1932–1972), conducted on Black men in Alabama who were deliberately left untreated for syphilis after penicillin became available, represents the most infamous American example of a pattern common in colonial medicine: treating colonized or subordinated populations as experimental subjects without consent.

Yet the history of colonial medicine is also a history of resistance. Indigenous healers adapted, concealed, and preserved traditional knowledge. Anticolonial intellectuals—Frantz Fanon foremost among them—argued that the destruction of indigenous healing systems was not incidental to colonialism but central to its project of domination. Fanon, a psychiatrist, observed that colonial psychiatry pathologized normal responses to oppression and used medical authority to enforce social control.

The contemporary global health landscape bears these marks. International health institutions were often founded by the same imperial powers whose policies created the conditions for global health disparities. The extraction of traditional plant-based knowledge for pharmaceutical development, without acknowledgment or compensation to indigenous communities, continues under the rubric of 'bioprospecting.' Debates over intellectual property, the TRIPS agreement, and access to essential medicines recapitulate colonial patterns of whose lives are deemed to have economic value.

questions.text: The passage claims that tropical medicine emerged as a discipline primarily to:
options: Advance universal scientific knowledge about infectious disease
Manage the health of European bodies in tropical environments and maintain the productivity of colonized labor forces — produced in and through colonialism rather than arising independently of it
Provide healthcare to indigenous populations in colonized territories
Study the ecological determinants of disease in tropical ecosystems
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Tropical medicine emerged as a discipline precisely to manage the health of European bodies in tropical environments and, increasingly, to maintain the productivity of colonized labor forces.'

questions.text: The Tuskegee study is cited in the passage as an example of:
options: The failure of medical ethics to keep pace with scientific advances

A pattern common in colonial medicine of treating colonized or subordinated populations as experimental subjects without consent — deliberately leaving Black men untreated for syphilis after penicillin became available
An isolated incident of American medical misconduct unrelated to colonialism
The limits of penicillin as a treatment for syphilis
questions.explanation: The passage presents Tuskegee as 'the most infamous American example of a pattern common in colonial medicine: treating colonized or subordinated populations as experimental subjects without consent.'

questions.text: Frantz Fanon's critique of colonial psychiatry argued that it:
options: Helped colonized populations by treating their mental illness

Pathologized normal responses to oppression and used medical authority to enforce social control — the destruction of indigenous healing systems was not incidental to colonialism but central to its project of domination
Was scientifically superior to indigenous healing practices
Only applied to French colonial psychiatry, not other colonial contexts
questions.explanation: The passage states Fanon 'argued that the destruction of indigenous healing systems was not incidental to colonialism but central to its project of domination' and 'observed that colonial psychiatry pathologized normal responses to oppression and used medical authority to enforce social control.'

questions.text: The passage's reference to 'bioprospecting' concerns which contemporary issue?
options: The exploration of tropical ecosystems for new species

The extraction of traditional plant-based knowledge from indigenous communities for pharmaceutical development without acknowledgment or compensation — continuing colonial patterns of whose knowledge has economic value
The international collaboration on vaccine development
The use of GPS technology to map disease vectors
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'The extraction of traditional plant-based knowledge for pharmaceutical development, without acknowledgment or compensation to indigenous communities, continues under the rubric of bioprospecting.'

questions.text: According to the passage, race science in colonial medicine served which function?
options: To accurately document genuine biological differences between human populations
To supposedly prove biological hierarchies — measuring skulls and cataloguing 'racial' differences — that justified differential treatment, resource allocation, and colonial domination
To advance the understanding of genetic variation across populations
To challenge existing hierarchies and promote equal treatment
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'Physical anthropologists measured skulls and catalogued racial differences in disease susceptibility, supposedly proving biological hierarchies that justified differential treatment and resource allocation.'

questions.text: The passage's overall argument about colonial medicine is best characterized as:
options: Colonial medicine was uniformly harmful and no good came from it

Colonial medicine had a 'dual face' — genuine scientific advances and disease amelioration occurred alongside imposition of racial hierarchies, extraction without consent, and destruction of indigenous healing systems; understanding this history is essential for contemporary global health
Modern global health institutions have successfully overcome colonial legacies
The solution to colonial medical harms is to reject Western medicine entirely
questions.explanation: The passage states colonial medicine had 'the dual face of Western scientific expansion: the genuine amelioration of disease and suffering, alongside the imposition of racial hierarchies, extraction of biological knowledge, and destruction of indigenous healing systems.' Contemporary global health 'bears these marks.'

passageText:
The question of whether there can be objective moral truths—and if so, what grounds them—has divided ethicists for centuries. Moral realism holds that some moral claims are objectively true or false, independent of what any individual or culture believes. Moral anti-realism encompasses several positions that deny this, including expressivism, error theory, and relativism.

Moral realists face the challenge of explaining what moral facts are and how we know them. G.E. Moore's 'open question argument' attempted to show that moral properties like 'goodness' cannot be reduced to natural properties like 'pleasure' or 'evolutionary fitness': it is always an open question whether something that is pleasant is thereby good. Moore concluded that goodness is a simple, indefinable, non-natural property known through a kind of moral intuition. Non-naturalist realists like Moore purchase objectivity at the cost of metaphysical queerness—a realm of non-natural moral facts floating free of the physical world.

Naturalist moral realists attempt to identify moral properties with natural properties while escaping the open question argument. Cornell realists argue that moral properties (like goodness) are natural properties but not definable in advance—they must be discovered empirically, like other natural properties. Moral knowledge is then continuous with scientific knowledge. But critics object that this still cannot close the gap between 'is' and 'ought': even a complete description of the natural world seems to leave it open what we ought to do.

Error theorists (J.L. Mackie foremost among them) accept the realist analysis of moral language—moral claims do purport to describe objective facts—but argue that no such facts exist. Moral judgments are therefore systematically false. We speak as if there are moral facts, but there aren't. Mackie found the metaphysical queerness of moral facts (their non-naturalness) and our supposed access to them (through what faculty?) implausible.

Expressivists avoid the problem of moral facts by denying that moral statements are truth-apt descriptions at all. 'Lying is wrong' expresses an attitude (disapproval, a commitment not to lie) rather than describing a fact about the world. Sophisticated expressivists like Simon Blackburn (quasi-realism) attempt to 'earn the right' to moral realist talk—truth, facts, knowledge—by showing that these terms, properly understood, are compatible with expressivist commitments.

The debate matters for practical ethics: if moral realism is true, we can aspire to genuine moral knowledge and progress; moral reform is correction, not merely change. If anti-realism is true, moral 'progress' may be reconceived as attitudinal change or construction rather than discovery of pre-existing truths.

questions.text: Moore's 'open question argument' is designed to show that:
options: Moral claims are always false because no moral facts exist

Moral properties like 'goodness' cannot be identified with natural properties — it is always coherently askable whether something that is pleasant (or fitness-enhancing) is thereby good, showing goodness is not reducible to natural properties
All ethical inquiry should proceed empirically like natural science
Moral intuition is unreliable as a source of knowledge
questions.explanation: The passage states Moore's argument attempted to show 'that moral properties like goodness cannot be reduced to natural properties like pleasure or evolutionary fitness: it is always an open question whether something that is pleasant is thereby good.'

questions.text: Mackie's error theory differs from expressivism in that error theory:
options: Denies that moral statements are intended to describe facts

Accepts that moral claims are meant to describe objective facts but argues those facts don't exist — all moral judgments are systematically false, unlike expressivism which holds moral statements express attitudes rather than making truth-apt claims
Holds that moral facts are natural properties discovered empirically
Argues that moral knowledge is possible through intuition
questions.explanation: The passage states error theorists 'accept the realist analysis of moral language—moral claims do purport to describe objective facts—but argue that no such facts exist. Moral judgments are therefore systematically false.' Expressivists deny moral statements are truth-apt descriptions at all.

options: Refute expressivism by showing that moral facts do exist

Show that an expressivist can legitimately use moral realist language (truth, facts, knowledge) — 'earning the right' to that vocabulary by explaining what these terms properly mean in an expressivist framework
Develop a naturalist account of moral properties
Prove that moral progress involves discovering pre-existing truths
questions.explanation: The passage states Blackburn and 'sophisticated expressivists...attempt to earn the right to moral realist talk—truth, facts, knowledge—by showing that these terms, properly understood, are compatible with expressivist commitments.'

questions.text: The passage notes that Cornell realists cannot fully escape the is-ought gap because:
options: They rely on non-natural moral facts

Even a complete empirical description of the natural world leaves it open what we ought to do — the gap between factual description and normative obligation cannot be closed by empirical discovery alone
Their account of moral properties contradicts evolutionary theory
They identify goodness with pleasure, falling prey to the open question argument
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'critics object that this still cannot close the gap between is and ought: even a complete description of the natural world seems to leave it open what we ought to do.'

questions.text: Based on the passage, which practical implication distinguishes moral realism from moral anti-realism?
options: Realism implies that moral rules must be enforced by law; anti-realism implies that law is unnecessary

If moral realism is true, moral reform is genuine correction toward pre-existing truths and moral progress is possible; if anti-realism is true, moral change is attitudinal change or construction rather than discovery — 'progress' means something different
Anti-realism allows more moral flexibility; realism demands moral uniformity across cultures
Only realism permits moral disagreement; anti-realism treats all moral views as equally valid
questions.explanation: The passage states: 'if moral realism is true, we can aspire to genuine moral knowledge and progress; moral reform is correction, not merely change. If anti-realism is true, moral progress may be reconceived as attitudinal change or construction rather than discovery of pre-existing truths.'

questions.text: Mackie's objections to moral realism centered on two related concerns. These were:
options: The impossibility of cultural agreement on moral norms, and the failure of religious justification for morality
The metaphysical queerness of non-natural moral facts (what kind of things would they be?) and epistemic queerness (through what faculty would we know them?) — both found implausible by Mackie
The evolutionary origin of moral intuitions, and the brain's inability to perceive abstract entities
The relativity of moral beliefs across cultures, and the impracticality of moral realist ethics
questions.explanation: The passage states Mackie 'found the metaphysical queerness of moral facts (their non-naturalness) and our supposed access to them (through what faculty?) implausible.' These are the two prongs of his 'argument from queerness.'
